proofs.proof_tex:
Proof. Fix alpha <= 1 and a law P in the stated boundary class, and impose the same fixed-truncation side conditions as in thm:local-experiment. First fix u_0 in (0,1]. The process on [u_0,1] is H_nP_u0(u)=n^{-1} sum_{i=1}^n {G_{n,P,u}(O_i)-E_P G_{n,P,u}(O)}. The hypotheses are exactly the hypotheses under which thm:local-experiment gives tightness of H_nP_u0 in D([u_0,1]). Thus there is no fixed-domain failure: every strictly positive truncation point gives a tight process.

Consider now the behavior as the truncation approaches the singular boundary u=0. In the case alpha=1, the annulus-oscillation hypothesis states that, for every sufficiently small delta_0>0, one can find indices u_n,v_n in (0,delta_0] and constants eta>0 and epsilon>0 such that liminf_n P_P(|H_n(u_n)-H_n(v_n)|>eta) >= epsilon. Since |H_n(u_n)-H_n(v_n)| is bounded above by sup_{u,v in (0,delta_0]} |H_n(u)-H_n(v)|, it follows that limsup_n P_P(sup_{u,v in (0,delta_0]} |H_n(u)-H_n(v)|>eta) >= epsilon. Letting delta_0 down to zero gives the displayed failure of uniform asymptotic equicontinuity at zero. This proves the alpha=1 nonuniformity clause and shows that the logarithmic Gaussian limits are fixed-truncation limits rather than a single equicontinuous process limit at u=0.

Finally suppose 0<alpha<1. By thm:local-experiment, for every fixed u_0 in (0,1] and finite u_1,...,u_m in [u_0,1], the vector (R_nP,H_nP_u0(u_1),...,H_nP_u0(u_m)) converges to the infinitely divisible extreme-shell/truncated-shell law. The assumed nonzero extreme-shell jump projection makes the limiting first coordinate nondegenerate. If R_nP were o_P(1), then R_nP would converge weakly to the point mass at zero. This contradicts convergence of the first coordinate to a nondegenerate infinitely divisible law. Hence R_nP is not o_P(1). Because R_nP is exactly the normalized empirical contribution of the omitted extreme shell 0<B<=n^{-1}, this contribution is first order in the joint decomposition and cannot be absorbed into the fixed-truncation process H_nP_u0 as u_0 downarrow 0. The proposition deliberately stops at this scalar extreme-shell statement; it does not assert a generic full-domain ell_infinity impossibility or a scalar endpoint omitted-shell result beyond the stated inputs.

Proof. Fix alpha in (0,infinity) and delta in (0,1). The causal target is theta_L=Gamma(P), while the estimator is the empirical endpoint hat_theta_L_n=Gamma(hat_P_n). The handle defines the scalar statistic T_{n,alpha}=rho_n(alpha)(hat_theta_L_n-theta_L), a phase-specific calibration law estimator hat L_{n,alpha}, its generalized quantiles hat q_-(alpha) and hat q_+(alpha) at levels delta/2 and 1-delta/2, and the interval C_n_alpha=[hat_theta_L_n-rho_n(alpha)^{-1}hat q_+(alpha), hat_theta_L_n-rho_n(alpha)^{-1}hat q_-(alpha)]. This is an explicit sample map once hat L_{n,alpha} is supplied.

The interval event is exactly a scalar quantile event. Indeed, theta_L belongs to C_n_alpha if and only if hat theta_L_n-rho_n(alpha)^{-1}hat q_+(alpha) <= theta_L <= hat theta_L_n-rho_n(alpha)^{-1}hat q_-(alpha), which is equivalent after multiplying by rho_n(alpha)>0 to hat q_-(alpha) <= T_{n,alpha} <= hat q_+(alpha). Let F_{n,P}(t)=P_P(T_{n,alpha}<=t). On the event that the random distribution estimator is within epsilon of F_{n,P} in Kolmogorov distance, the generalized quantile inequalities imply P_P(hat q_-(alpha) <= T_{n,alpha} <= hat q_+(alpha)) >= 1-delta-2 epsilon, up to the probability that the calibration event fails. The fixed-alpha calibrator assumption makes this calibration error vanish uniformly over P in P_n(alpha). Therefore liminf_{n to infinity} inf_{P in P_n(alpha)} P_P(theta_L in C_n_alpha) >= 1-delta.

For length, the same definition gives |C_n_alpha|=rho_n(alpha)^{-1}{hat q_+(alpha)-hat q_-(alpha)}. The fixed-alpha calibrator assumption includes the uniform bound E_P[hat q_+(alpha)-hat q_-(alpha)]=O(1). Taking suprema over P in P_n(alpha) yields sup_{P in P_n(alpha)} E_P|C_n_alpha|=O(rho_n(alpha)^{-1}).

The alpha=1 transition is covered without a separate argument. In that regime def:alpha-normalization gives rho_n(1)={n/(1+log n)}^{1/2}, thm:endpoint-limit-law supplies the logarithmic scalar endpoint law for T_{n,1}, and the calibrator assumption is imposed for this fixed alpha. Thus the same scalar quantile interval has asymptotic coverage at least 1-delta and expected length of order rho_n(1)^{-1}. This proves the conditional fixed-alpha CI result. The proof does not construct hat L_{n,alpha} from primitive CCOT objects; that construction is exactly what the disclosed calibrator condition supplies.
